ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tempeh entry (FOODON_03315671); no specific fried tempeh platter entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers tempeh as a soy-based fermented product; no specific deep-fried preparation code exists. Closest parent match used with deep-frying preparation context noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried tempeh platter. SNOMED CT food substance coverage for this specific preparation is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is six pieces of fried tempeh weighing approximately 180 g, representing the tempeh pieces without sauce; a full platter with peanut sauce is approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tempeh, cooked (SR Legacy ID 16114); adapted for deep-fried preparation using published frying oil absorption coefficients
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked tempeh (FDC ID 16114) provides the base macronutrient profile. Fat content is adjusted upward to reflect deep-frying oil absorption (approximately +8–10 g fat per 100 g versus steamed/boiled tempeh), consistent with published frying absorption coefficients in peer-reviewed food science literature. Sodium is elevated to reflect seasoning and batter. Input record values (grabfood, confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) are broadly consistent with this adapted profile and have been retained with minor rounding normalization. USDA FoodData Central is the sole approved primary source for nutrient values in this record.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried battered tempeh platter in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on: (1) plain tempeh GI reported as approximately 41–55 in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), a peer-reviewed source indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database; (2) addition of wheat flour batter and deep-frying raises effective GI toward the medium boundary, but the high protein content (11.7 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) moderate postprandial glucose response and keep the estimate within the low GI range; (3) GI 55 is the upper bound of the peer-reviewed tempeh range and is used conservatively. Glycemic load per 180 g serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (10.0 g/100 g × 180 g = 18.0 g) / 100 = 9.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 267 mg/100 g; deep-fried preparation with palm oil; peanut sauce adds additional sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 90–120 g of tempeh pieces, request peanut sauce on the side or omit it, and account for the approximately 480 mg sodium contribution from a full 180 g serving when planning daily sodium intake against the recommended ceiling of 2000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (10 g/100 g) with low estimated GI (~55); protein and fibre moderate postprandial glucose response; peanut sauce adds palm sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may consume a moderate portion (90–120 g of tempeh pieces) as the protein (11.7 g/100 g) and fibre (2.0 g/100 g) slow gastric emptying, but should request peanut sauce on the side or omit it to avoid the additional refined sugar and carbohydrate load that can elevate postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying in palm oil elevates saturated fat and total fat; trans fat present at 0.2 g/100 g; sodium 267 mg/100 g; tempeh soy isoflavones are cardioprotective but offset by frying method
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of deep-fried tempeh due to elevated total fat (15 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.7 g/100 g), and detectable trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from the frying process; steamed or pan-seared tempeh preparations are preferred to preserve the cardioprotective soy isoflavone benefits without the atherogenic lipid burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; palm oil contributes pro-inflammatory saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried tempeh preparations as the frying process generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; steamed or pan-seared tempeh is recommended to retain the anti-inflammatory benefits of fermented soy without the pro-inflammatory AGE burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Tempeh Platter (6 Pieces) is an Indonesian-origin snack dish consisting of six pieces of tempeh — a traditional fermented soybean cake — that have been coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried in palm oil until golden and crispy. Tempeh is made by culturing whole soybeans with Rhizopus mould, producing a firm, nutty-flavoured cake with a dense, slightly chewy interior and a crisp fried exterior. The dish is widely served as a snack or side dish in Indonesian cuisine and is popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 217 kcal, 11.7 g protein, 15.0 g fat (2.7 g saturated), 10.0 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 267 mg sodium. The high protein content reflects the soybean base, while the elevated fat content results from oil absorption during deep-frying. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Clinically, the sodium level (267 mg/100 g) is moderate and warrants portion control for individuals managing hypertension. The low estimated glycemic index (~55) and moderate carbohydrate content make it acceptable for type 2 diabetes management in controlled portions, particularly without peanut sauce. The deep-frying method generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and elevates saturated fat, which are concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. Tempeh's soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) provide cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory properties that are partially offset by the frying preparation. The dish is halal, vegan, and gluten-containing due to the wheat flour batter. A standard serving is six pieces (approximately 180 g) without sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried tempeh?
Is fried tempeh high in sodium?
Can I eat fried tempeh if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein in fried tempeh?
What is the glycemic index of tempeh?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked tempeh (FDC ID 16114) with fat adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption and sodium adjusted for seasoning and batter. The input record (grabfood, confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) provides broadly consistent values. No exact approved-source entry for deep-fried battered tempeh platter exists; the adaptation is well-documented and defensible but not an exact match, warranting medium confidence.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for deep-fried battered tempeh is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 16114) as of 2024; the frying medium (palm oil) is predominantly saturated and monounsaturated but exact post-frying absorbed fatty acid fractions are not available from the approved source. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific fried preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ID 16114. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific deep-fried battered tempeh platter preparation are not available in the adapted USDA source consulted. Input record ethnic_tags contained erroneous entries ('Stuffed Pancake', 'Pancake', 'Coffee') inconsistent with fried tempeh; these have been discarded. country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore; corrected to Indonesia as the dish's culinary origin, noting it is widely served in Singapore. Two default portions in input (both marked is_default: true) noted; 180 g (6 pieces without sauce) adopted as primary serving size for nutrient calculations. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283, peer-reviewed); no direct GI measurement for deep-fried battered tempeh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. Glycemic load per 180 g serving = (55 × 18.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.9 g. Nutrient source is exclusively USDA FoodData Central (approved primary source); no forbidden sources used as primary evidence.
